missionStatement: Morning Edition is a shared front page for San Diego — built to help us notice more, understand where we live, participate in our community, and have better conversations.

frontPage.headline: San Diego City Council Moves Forward on Affordable Housing Zoning Reform
frontPage.summary: The San Diego City Council voted 6–3 Tuesday to advance a zoning reform package that would allow higher-density housing along major transit corridors. The proposal, part of the city's Climate Action Plan update, would upzone stretches of El Cajon Boulevard, University Avenue, and parts of Midway. A final vote is expected in August.
frontPage.whyItMatters: San Diego's housing shortage drives some of the highest rents in the country. This zoning change, if passed, would be among the most significant in the city's history — potentially unlocking tens of thousands of new units near existing bus and trolley lines, reducing car dependency and long commutes for residents who can't afford to live close to work.

sources.label: City of San Diego Council Agenda

whySelected.note: This affects every renter and homeowner in the city. The August vote is the next concrete moment to watch.

frontPage.conversationStarter: Would you want a 6-story apartment building on your block if it meant cheaper rent citywide?

aroundTown.title: Ocean Beach Street Fair & Chili Cook-Off Returns This Weekend
aroundTown.description: One of San Diego's oldest neighborhood street fairs closes Newport Avenue to cars and fills it with local vendors, live music on multiple stages, and the beloved chili cook-off. The event draws tens of thousands of visitors each year and is free to attend.
aroundTown.whyCare: If you've never been to an OB Street Fair, it's one of those San Diego experiences that's genuinely unlike anything else. It's loud, it's chaotic, it smells like chili and sunscreen, and it's exactly what Newport Ave was designed for.
aroundTown.neighborhoodNudge: Worth leaving your neighborhood for if you want a loud, chaotic, very San Diego Saturday. OB rewards people who show up with no agenda.

ifYouGo.bestTime: Late afternoon into sunset — the energy peaks around 4–7pm and the lighting gets gorgeous
ifYouGo.parking: Very difficult. Do not expect easy street parking near Newport Avenue. Plan to park several blocks away and walk, or don't drive at all.
ifYouGo.transit: No trolley runs into Ocean Beach. If using public transit, verify current MTS bus routes before you go at sdmts.com.
ifYouGo.cost: Free to attend. Bring cash for vendors and food.
ifYouGo.goodFor: Friends, casual wandering, local food, live music, people watching
ifYouGo.insiderTip: Wear comfortable shoes and bring a light jacket — OB gets cold fast after dark even in June.

tags: Strong reason to visit another neighborhood
whySelected.note: OB Street Fair is one of those San Diego events most locals have heard of but never quite made it to.

aroundTown.conversationStarter: Would you rather deal with impossible parking or pay for the Uber?

lookingAhead.title: Big Bay Boom — July 4th Fireworks Over San Diego Bay
lookingAhead.description: The Port of San Diego's Big Bay Boom returns for the nation's 250th birthday — fireworks launched simultaneously from barges positioned across the bay off Shelter Island, Harbor Island, Embarcadero North, the Marina District, and Coronado Ferry Landing. The synchronized show lasts roughly 18 minutes and is visible from multiple waterfront neighborhoods. This year's display is expected to be especially large.
lookingAhead.decisionDeadline: By Wednesday July 2 — pick your viewing spot now. Waterfront areas fill up well before the 9:15 PM show.
lookingAhead.goodFor: Families, couples, groups — anyone who wants to be part of something the whole city turns out for. One of the few events with this kind of shared-moment energy.
lookingAhead.gettingThere: MTS is running expanded Trolley service specifically for the Big Bay Boom, with service to stations near waterfront viewing areas including County Center/Little Italy, America Plaza, Seaport Village, and Convention Center. Driving downtown on July 4th is not recommended — parking fills hours before the show.
lookingAhead.parkingNote: Free MTS park-and-ride lots are available throughout the system. Park outside downtown and ride the Trolley in. Do not plan to drive to the Embarcadero.
lookingAhead.transitNote: MTS runs expanded Trolley service on July 4th for the Big Bay Boom. Stations near prime viewing areas: County Center/Little Italy, America Plaza, Seaport Village, Convention Center, Gaslamp Quarter. On July 4th, each paid fare gets a friend in free. Verify extended hours and schedules at sdmts.com before you go.
lookingAhead.rideshareNote: Rideshare is fine for arrival. Expect significant surge pricing and long waits after the show — the Trolley home is strongly recommended.
lookingAhead.insiderTip: Harbor Island and Shelter Island offer bay-level views with more room to breathe than the Embarcadero. Worth the extra few minutes to get there if you want a calmer spot.
lookingAhead.conversationStarter: Do you actually enjoy big public fireworks shows, or do you mostly endure them?

sources.label: MTS — Big Bay Boom Rider Info
Port of San Diego — Big Bay Boom 2026

tags: Strong reason to visit another neighborhood
whySelected.note: July 4th on the nation's 250th birthday — the event of the week. Requires real planning: picking a viewing spot, figuring out transit, deciding how late to stay. The MTS trolley details are real and useful to include once verified.

storyline.topic: San Diego FC's First MLS Season
storyline.headline: San Diego FC Is Already Rewriting What a Startup MLS Club Can Look Like
storyline.body:
San Diego FC kicked off its inaugural MLS season this spring, and it has been anything but a typical expansion year. Backed by the Sycuan Band of the Kumeyaay Nation and the Chelsea FC ownership group, the club arrived with serious financial backing, a sold-out stadium, and a head coach in Mikey Varas who built his reputation coaching the U.S. youth national team.

What's surprised observers is the performance. Expansion teams are expected to struggle. San Diego FC has not cooperated with that expectation. Their attack is fast and fun to watch, the fanbase showed up with genuine intensity from day one, and Snapdragon Stadium has become one of the best matchday environments in the league.

The storyline the league is watching: can San Diego FC sustain this? MLS expansion clubs often have honeymoon seasons and then fall back to earth as opponents figure out their patterns. The second half of the season will tell us whether this club has the depth to compete — or whether they're riding early-season energy.

For locals, the story is less about MLS and more about what it means to have a major professional sports team that's actually here, playing in a stadium designed for soccer, in a city that has wanted this for a long time.

whySelected.note: San Diego FC is one of the better local stories of the year. Worth keeping readers up to speed as the season progresses.

storyline.conversationStarter: Have you been to a San Diego FC game yet? Is Snapdragon Stadium worth the trip from your neighborhood?

worthYourTime.title: The Death and Life of a Great American City's Last Bookstore
worthYourTime.author: Kyle Chayka
worthYourTime.teaser: A meditation on what independent bookstores mean to urban neighborhoods, and why so many are finding ways to survive despite every prediction that they wouldn't. Chayka profiles three stores in three cities and asks what they actually are — retail shops, community centers, or something harder to name. Worth reading slowly with coffee.

whySelected.note: San Diego has several beloved independent bookstores — this piece gives you useful context to appreciate them differently.

miniPuzzle.prompt: San Diego's Gaslamp Quarter is named for the ornate street lamps installed during the district's Victorian-era heyday. But what was the neighborhood's less flattering nickname before it was redeveloped in the 1970s and '80s?
miniPuzzle.hint: It shares a name with a type of ray found in San Diego Bay.

whySelected.note: The Gaslamp's history is much wilder than its current vibe suggests.

hallwayQuestion.question: Are In-N-Out fries underrated, overrated, or exactly rated?
hallwayQuestion.note: We're not starting a fight. We're starting a conversation.

editorNote: First edition of Morning Edition — San Diego. Thanks for reading.
